content:
# Joshua Chapter 11 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 235-236 | Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 169-170 (LXX)
**Source Images:** `../../source-texts/hebrew-OT/JOS_011_page_001.jpg`, `../../source-texts/hebrew-OT/JOS_011_page_002.jpg`; `../../source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/JOS_011_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `../../source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/JOS_011_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

### The Northern Coalition Against Israel (vv. 1-5)

**1** And it came to pass, when Jabin king of Hazor heard, that he sent to Jobab king of Madon, and to the king of Shimron, and to the king of Achshaph,

**2** and to the kings who were in the north, in the hill country, and in the Arabah south of Chinneroth, and in the lowland, and in the heights of Dor to the west,

**3** the Canaanite to the east and to the west, and the Amorite, and the Hittite, and the Perizzite, and the Jebusite in the hill country, and the Hivite beneath Hermon in the land of Mizpah.

**4** And they went out, they and all their camps with them, a people as numerous as the sand that is on the seashore in multitude, with very many horses and chariots.

**5** And all these kings met together by appointment, and they came and encamped together at the waters of Merom, to fight against Israel.

---

### YHWH's Command and Joshua's Victory (vv. 6-9)

**6** And YHWH said to Joshua, "Do not be afraid before them, for tomorrow at this time I am giving all of them slain before Israel. You shall hamstring their horses and burn their chariots with fire."

**7** And Joshua, and all the people of war with him, came against them by the waters of Merom suddenly, and they fell upon them.

**8** And YHWH gave them into the hand of Israel, and they struck them and pursued them as far as Great Sidon, and as far as Misrephoth-maim, and as far as the valley of Mizpeh to the east. And they struck them until no survivor was left to them.

**9** And Joshua did to them just as YHWH had said to him: he hamstrung their horses and burned their chariots with fire.

---

### The Capture and Destruction of Hazor (vv. 10-15)

**10** And Joshua turned back at that time and captured Hazor, and struck its king with the sword, for Hazor formerly was the head of all these kingdoms.

**11** And they struck every soul that was in it with the edge of the sword, utterly devoting them to destruction. There was nothing left that breathed. And he burned Hazor with fire.

**12** And all the cities of these kings, and all their kings, Joshua captured, and he struck them with the edge of the sword, utterly devoting them to destruction, just as Moses the servant of YHWH had commanded.

**13** Only, all the cities that were standing on their tells, Israel did not burn them — except Hazor alone; that one Joshua burned.

**14** And all the spoil of these cities, and the livestock, the Sons of Israel plundered for themselves. Only, every human being they struck with the edge of the sword until they had destroyed them; they did not leave anything that breathed.

**15** As YHWH commanded Moses his servant, so Moses commanded Joshua, and so Joshua did. He did not turn aside a word from all that YHWH had commanded Moses.

---

### Summary of the Conquered Lands (vv. 16-20)

**16** And Joshua took all this land: the hill country, and all the Negev, and all the land of Goshen, and the lowland, and the Arabah, and the hill country of Israel and its lowland,

**17** from Mount Halak, which goes up toward Seir, as far as Baal-gad in the valley of Lebanon beneath Mount Hermon. And all their kings he captured, and struck them, and put them to death.

**18** Joshua made war with all these kings many days.

**19** There was not a city that made peace with the Sons of Israel except the Hivites dwelling in Gibeon. They took all in battle.

**20** For it was from YHWH to harden their hearts, to meet Israel in battle, in order that they might be utterly devoted to destruction, that they might receive no mercy, but that he might destroy them, just as YHWH had commanded Moses.

---

### The Anakim Cut Off (vv. 21-23)

**21** And Joshua came at that time and cut off the Anakim from the hill country, from Hebron, from Debir, from Anab, and from all the hill country of Judah, and from all the hill country of Israel. With their cities, Joshua utterly devoted them to destruction.

**22** No Anakim were left in the land of the Sons of Israel. Only in Gaza, in Gath, and in Ashdod did they remain.

**23** And Joshua took all the land, according to all that YHWH had spoken to Moses. And Joshua gave it as an inheritance to Israel according to their divisions by their tribes. And the land had rest from war.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Transliteration & Divine Name
- **YHWH** (יהוה): Retained as the Tetragrammaton throughout, per LOGOS convention. Not rendered "the LORD." Appears in vv. 6, 8, 9, 12, 15 (x2), 20 (x2), 23.
- **Sons of Israel** (בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל): Retained literally in vv. 14, 19, 22 per LOGOS policy; not "children of Israel" or "Israelites."

### Key Lexical Decisions
- **"utterly devoting them to destruction"** (הַחֲרֵם, vv. 11, 12, 14, 20, 21): The Hiphil infinitive absolute of חרם (*herem*), denoting total consecration to YHWH through destruction. This is a technical term of holy-war theology. The LXX renders it with forms of ἐξολεθρεύω and ἀνάθεμα. "Utterly devoting to destruction" preserves both the totality and the sacral nature of the act, avoiding the softer "completely destroyed."
- **"hamstring"** (עִקֵּר, vv. 6, 9): The verb עקר means to sever the hamstring tendons of horses, rendering them permanently lame. This was standard ancient Near Eastern practice to neutralize captured chariotry without incorporating enemy military assets. YHWH's command ensures Israel relies on divine power rather than military technology (cf. Deut 17:16).
- **"their tells"** (תִּלָּם, v. 13): Hebrew תֵּל (*tel*), the artificial mound formed by successive layers of occupation. The plural construct תִּלָּם ("their tells") is retained as a technical archaeological/geographical term rather than the interpretive "their mounds." The point is that most conquered cities' ruins were left standing, except Hazor.
- **"the head"** (הָרֹאשׁ, v. 10): Literally "the head" — Hazor was the chief city, the political capital of the northern coalition. Confirmed by archaeological evidence: Hazor was the largest Canaanite city in the Late Bronze Age (200 acres).

### Geographical Notes
- **Hazor** (חָצוֹר, v. 1): Identified with Tell el-Qedah, approximately 10 miles north of the Sea of Galilee. The largest fortified city in Canaan, ruling a network of vassal kingdoms. Named in the Mari archives and the Amarna letters.
- **Chinneroth** (כִנְּרוֹת, v. 2): The Sea of Galilee, also called Gennesaret. The name derives from כִּנּוֹר (*kinnor*, "harp/lyre"), perhaps from the lake's shape.
- **Waters of Merom** (מֵי מֵרוֹם, v. 5): Traditionally identified with Lake Huleh (now drained), north of the Sea of Galilee, though some scholars place it at Tell el-Khirbeh near Meiron.
- **Great Sidon** (צִידוֹן רַבָּה, v. 8): The major Phoenician coastal city, modern Saida in Lebanon. The epithet "great" (רַבָּה) distinguishes it as the metropolis.
- **Misrephoth-maim** (מִשְׂרְפוֹת מַיִם, v. 8): Literally "burnings of waters," perhaps hot springs or a place of lime-burning near the coast. Located between Sidon and Acco.
- **Mount Halak** (הַר הֶחָלָק, v. 17): Literally "the smooth/bald mountain," marking the southern boundary of conquest. Located near the ascent to Seir (Edom).
- **Baal-gad** (בַּעַל גָּד, v. 17): Marks the northern extent of conquest, in the Beqaa Valley at the foot of Mount Hermon. The name means "Lord of Fortune."
- **Gaza, Gath, Ashdod** (v. 22): Three of the five cities of the Philistine Pentapolis where the Anakim survived. This note foreshadows the Philistine conflicts in Judges and Samuel.

### Textual Notes
- **v. 1 — Jabin (יָבִין)**: The name means "he discerns/perceives." A Jabin also appears as king of Hazor in Judges 4:2, likely a dynastic name or title.
- **v. 13 — cities standing on their tells**: The MT reads עַל־תִּלָּם, "on their tell(s)." The LXX (Vaticanus) renders τὰς κεχωματισμένας, "the mounded [cities]," confirming the sense of cities built upon accumulated ruins.
- **v. 20 — "to harden their hearts"**: Hebrew לְחַזֵּק אֶת־לִבָּם, literally "to make strong their heart." The Piel of חזק denotes divine hardening, a theological parallel to the hardening of Pharaoh's heart (Exod 4:21; 9:12). The LXX renders ἰσχῦσαι αὐτῶν τὴν καρδίαν. This verse provides the theological explanation for the total war: the Canaanites' intransigence was itself part of YHWH's sovereign plan.
- **v. 21 — the Anakim** (הָעֲנָקִים): A people of extraordinary stature whose presence in Canaan terrified the spies in Numbers 13:28, 33. Their elimination by Joshua resolves the narrative tension from the wilderness period — the very giants Israel once feared are now defeated.

### Structural Note
Joshua 11 forms the climax of the conquest narrative (chs. 1-12). Chapter 10 recounted the southern campaign; chapter 11 recounts the northern campaign. The chapter moves from specific battle (vv. 1-9) to regional summary (vv. 10-15) to total-land summary (vv. 16-23). The concluding formula "and the land had rest from war" (v. 23) closes the military section and transitions to the allotment of territory (chs. 13-21). The phrase deliberately echoes YHWH's promise of "rest" (מְנוּחָה) in Deuteronomy 12:10 and Joshua 1:13.
